# REMOVE | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/remove.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/remove.html#remove)
REMOVE
==============================================================

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/remove.html#example-graph)
Example graph
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

    CREATE
        (billy :Player {name: 'Billy', score: 84}),
        (andy :Player {name: 'Andy', score: 21}),
        (lori :Player:Admin {name: 'Lori', score: 90})
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/remove.html#remove-attributes)
Remove attributes
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The following query removes the ‘score’ attribute from the node representing Andy.

    MATCH (n {name: 'Andy'})
    REMOVE n.score
    RETURN n.name, n.score
    

Result:

| n.name | n.score |
| --- | --- |
| “Andy” | Null |

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/remove.html#remove-a-label-from-a-node)
Remove a label from a node
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

To remove a label from a node use the REMOVE clause as follows:

    MATCH (n {name: 'Lori'})
    REMOVE n:Admin
    RETURN n.name, labels(n)
    

Result:

| n.name | labels(n) |
| --- | --- |
| “Lori” | \[Player\] |

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/remove.html#removing-multiple-labels-from-a-node)
Removing multiple labels from a node
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Similar to removing a single label from a node we can use the REMOVE clause to remove multiple labels in one go

    MATCH (n :Player {name: 'Lori'})
    REMOVE n:Admin:Player
    RETURN n.name, labels(n)
    

Result:

| n.name | labels(n) |
| --- | --- |
| “Lori” | \[\] |

* * *

---

# Quick Start | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/genai-tools/mcpserver/quickstart.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/genai-tools/mcpserver/quickstart.html#quick-start)
Quick Start
===========================================================================================

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/genai-tools/mcpserver/quickstart.html#prerequisites)
Prerequisites
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Node.js 18+
*   FalkorDB instance (running locally or remotely)
*   Claude Desktop app (for AI integration)

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/genai-tools/mcpserver/quickstart.html#running-from-npm)
Running from npm
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Add to your Claude Desktop config (`~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json` on macOS):

    {
      "mcpServers": {
        "falkordb": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": [\
            "-y",\
            "@falkordb/mcpserver@latest"\
          ],
          "env": {
            "FALKORDB_HOST": "localhost",
            "FALKORDB_PORT": "6379",
            "FALKORDB_USERNAME": "",
            "FALKORDB_PASSWORD": ""
          }
        }
      }
    }
    

Restart Claude Desktop and you’ll see the FalkorDB tools available!

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/genai-tools/mcpserver/quickstart.html#running-with-npx)
Running with npx
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can run the server directly from the command line:

    # Run with stdio transport (default)
    FALKORDB_HOST=localhost FALKORDB_PORT=6379 npx -y @falkordb/mcpserver
    
    # Run with HTTP transport
    MCP_TRANSPORT=http MCP_PORT=3005 FALKORDB_HOST=localhost FALKORDB_PORT=6379 npx -y @falkordb/mcpserver
    
    # Using a .env file with dotenv-cli
    npx dotenv-cli -e .env -- npx @falkordb/mcpserver
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/genai-tools/mcpserver/quickstart.html#docker-compose)
Docker Compose
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Run FalkorDB and the MCP server together:

    git clone https://github.com/FalkorDB/FalkorDB-MCPServer.git
    cd FalkorDB-MCPServer
    cp .env.example .env   # Edit to set MCP_API_KEY, FALKORDB_PASSWORD, etc.
    docker compose up -d
    

This starts both FalkorDB and the MCP server with health checks and persistent volumes.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/genai-tools/mcpserver/quickstart.html#available-mcp-tools)
Available MCP Tools
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Once connected to Claude or another MCP-compatible AI assistant, you can:

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/genai-tools/mcpserver/quickstart.html#query-graphs)
Query Graphs

    "Show me all people who know each other"
    "Find the shortest path between two nodes"
    "What relationships does John have?"
    "Run a read-only query on the replica instance"
    

The `query_graph` tool supports a `readOnly` parameter to execute queries using `GRAPH.RO_QUERY`, ideal for:

*   Running queries on replica instances
*   Preventing accidental write operations
*   Ensuring data integrity in production environments

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/genai-tools/mcpserver/quickstart.html#manage-data)
Manage Data

    "Create a new person named Alice who knows Bob"
    "Add a 'WORKS_AT' relationship between Alice and TechCorp"
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/genai-tools/mcpserver/quickstart.html#explore-structure)
Explore Structure

    "List all available graphs"
    "Show me the structure of the user_data graph"
    "Delete the old_test graph"
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/genai-tools/mcpserver/quickstart.html#example-usage)
Example Usage
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Once connected, Claude can help you write and execute queries:

    // Query relationships
    MATCH (p:Person)-[:KNOWS]->(friend:Person)
    WHERE p.name = 'Alice'
    RETURN friend.name, friend.age
    
    // Create data structures
    CREATE (alice:Person {name: 'Alice', age: 30})
    CREATE (bob:Person {name: 'Bob', age: 25})
    CREATE (alice)-[:KNOWS {since: 2020}]->(bob)
    
    // Analyze your graph
    MATCH path = shortestPath((start:Person)-[*]-(end:Person))
    WHERE start.name = 'Alice' AND end.name = 'Charlie'
    RETURN path
    

* * *

---

# RedisGraph to FalkorDB | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/redisgraph-to-falkordb.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/redisgraph-to-falkordb.html#redisgraph-to-falkordb-migration)
RedisGraph to FalkorDB Migration
================================================================================================================================================

FalkorDB is fully compatible with RedisGraph RDB (Redis Database) files, making migration straightforward.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/redisgraph-to-falkordb.html#migration-steps)
Migration Steps
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/redisgraph-to-falkordb.html#step-1-create-rdb-snapshot-from-redisgraph)
Step 1: Create RDB Snapshot from RedisGraph

Create a snapshot of your RedisGraph database using the `SAVE` or `BGSAVE` command:

    # Connect to your RedisGraph instance
    redis-cli
    
    # Create snapshot (blocks server during save)
    SAVE
    
    # Or create snapshot in background (recommended for production)
    BGSAVE
    

This creates a `dump.rdb` file in your Redis data directory.

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/redisgraph-to-falkordb.html#step-2-load-rdb-file-into-falkordb)
Step 2: Load RDB File into FalkorDB

#### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/redisgraph-to-falkordb.html#using-docker)
Using Docker

When using the FalkorDB Docker image, mount your RDB file and configure Redis to load it:

       docker run -it -p 6379:6379 -v $(pwd):/data -e REDIS_ARGS="--dir /data --dbfilename dump.rdb" falkordb/falkordb
    

Make sure to place the RDB file in the directory mapped to the Docker volume. For FalkorDB Cloud, follow the cloud provider’s instructions for uploading and restoring from an RDB file.

**Important:** Ensure your `dump.rdb` file is placed in the directory mapped to the Docker volume (`$(pwd)` in the example above).

#### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/redisgraph-to-falkordb.html#using-falkordb-cloud)
Using FalkorDB Cloud

For FalkorDB Cloud deployments, follow the cloud provider’s instructions for uploading and restoring from an RDB file.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/redisgraph-to-falkordb.html#verification)
Verification
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

After loading the RDB file, verify your data:

    # Connect to FalkorDB
    redis-cli
    
    # List all graphs
    GRAPH.LIST
    
    # Query a graph to verify data
    GRAPH.QUERY your_graph_name "MATCH (n) RETURN count(n)"
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/redisgraph-to-falkordb.html#best-practices)
Best Practices
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Verify RDB Integrity:** Check the RDB file integrity before and after transfer
*   **Plan for Downtime:** Consider downtime requirements and data consistency during migration
*   **Test First:** Always test the migration in a staging environment before production
*   **Backup:** Keep backups of both the source RedisGraph data and the RDB file
*   **Monitor:** After migration, monitor FalkorDB performance and verify queries work correctly

* * *

---

# text.regexGroups | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/regexGroups.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/regexGroups.html#textregexgroups)
text.regexGroups
==============================================================================================

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/regexGroups.html#description)
Description
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Extracts all matches and capture groups from a string using a regular expression pattern. Returns a nested array where each match contains the full match and any captured groups.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/regexGroups.html#syntax)
Syntax
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

    flex.text.regexGroups(string, regex)
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/regexGroups.html#parameters)
Parameters
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `string` | string | Yes | The string to search in |
| `regex` | string | Yes | The regular expression pattern (applied globally) |

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/regexGroups.html#returns)
Returns
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Type:** list of lists

A nested array where each inner array represents one match and contains the full match followed by any capture groups. Returns `null` if input string is `null`.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/regexGroups.html#examples)
Examples
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/regexGroups.html#example-1-extract-email-components)
Example 1: Extract Email Components

    WITH 'Contact: john@example.com or jane@test.org' AS text
    RETURN flex.text.regexGroups(text, '(\\w+)@(\\w+\\.\\w+)') AS matches
    

**Output:**

    matches
    ----------------------------------------------------
    [["john@example.com", "john", "example.com"], \
     ["jane@test.org", "jane", "test.org"]]
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/regexGroups.html#example-2-parse-date-components)
Example 2: Parse Date Components

    WITH '2024-01-15 and 2024-12-25' AS dates
    RETURN flex.text.regexGroups(dates, '(\\d{4})-(\\d{2})-(\\d{2})') AS parsed
    

**Output:**

    parsed
    -------------------------------------------------
    [["2024-01-15", "2024", "01", "15"],\
     ["2024-12-25", "2024", "12", "25"]]
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/regexGroups.html#example-3-extract-urls-and-protocol)
Example 3: Extract URLs and Protocol

    MATCH (d:Document)
    WITH d, flex.text.regexGroups(d.content, '(https?)://([\\w.]+)') AS urls
    WHERE size(urls) > 0
    RETURN d.title, urls
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/regexGroups.html#notes)
Notes
-------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Returns `null` if input string is `null`
*   The regex is applied globally (finds all matches)
*   Each match array contains: \[fullMatch, group1, group2, …\]
*   Useful for parsing structured text, extracting data patterns
*   More powerful than simple string search for complex patterns

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/regexGroups.html#see-also)
See Also
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   [text.replace](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/replace.html)
     - Replace text using regex
*   [text.indexOf](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/indexOf.html)
     - Find simple substring position
*   [text.indexesOf](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/indexesOf.html)
     - Find all substring positions

* * *

---

# map.removeKey | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/map/removeKey.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/map/removeKey.html#mapremovekey)
map.removeKey
=====================================================================================

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/map/removeKey.html#description)
Description
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Creates a new map with a single specified key removed. The original map is not modified.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/map/removeKey.html#syntax)
Syntax
------------------------------------------------------------------------

    flex.map.removeKey(map, key)
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/map/removeKey.html#parameters)
Parameters
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `map` | map | Yes | The map to remove the key from |
| `key` | string | Yes | The key to remove |

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/map/removeKey.html#returns)
Returns
--------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Type:** map (object)

A new map containing all properties from the input map except the specified key. Returns an empty map if input is not a valid object.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/map/removeKey.html#examples)
Examples
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/map/removeKey.html#example-1-basic-key-removal)
Example 1: Basic Key Removal

    WITH {name: 'Alice', age: 30, email: 'alice@example.com'} AS user
    RETURN flex.map.removeKey(user, 'email') AS sanitized
    

**Output:**

    sanitized
    -----------------------
    {name: 'Alice', age: 30}
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/map/removeKey.html#example-2-removing-sensitive-data)
Example 2: Removing Sensitive Data

    MATCH (u:User)
    WITH properties(u) AS userProps
    RETURN u.id, flex.map.removeKey(userProps, 'password') AS safeProps
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/map/removeKey.html#example-3-cleaning-response-data)
Example 3: Cleaning Response Data

    MATCH (p:Product {id: 123})
    WITH properties(p) AS props
    WITH flex.map.removeKey(props, 'internalId') AS cleaned
    RETURN cleaned AS product
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/map/removeKey.html#example-4-removing-non-existent-key)
Example 4: Removing Non-Existent Key

    WITH {a: 1, b: 2} AS map
    RETURN flex.map.removeKey(map, 'c') AS result
    

**Output:**

    result
    ---------
    {a: 1, b: 2}
    

(Key doesn’t exist, so map is returned unchanged)

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/map/removeKey.html#notes)
Notes
----------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Returns empty map if input is not a valid object or is `null`
*   If key is `null`, returns a shallow copy of the map
*   If the key doesn’t exist, returns a copy with all original properties
*   Creates a new map; does not modify the original
*   Useful for sanitizing data, removing sensitive fields, or filtering properties

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/map/removeKey.html#see-also)
See Also
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   [map.removeKeys](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/map/removeKeys.html)
     - Remove multiple keys at once
*   [map.submap](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/map/submap.html)
     - Keep only specific keys (inverse operation)
*   [map.merge](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/map/merge.html)
     - Combine multiple maps

* * *

---

# Query History | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/browser/ui/query-history.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/browser/ui/query-history.html#query-history)
Query History
=======================================================================================

Query History stores previously executed queries in the browser (localStorage) and exposes a dialog to browse them.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/browser/ui/query-history.html#opening-query-history)
Opening Query History
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Click the history button (clock icon) in the selector bar.

If there are no saved queries, the button is disabled.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/browser/ui/query-history.html#what-you-can-do)
What you can do
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Inside the Query History dialog:

*   **Search** previous queries.
*   **Filter by graph name** (toggle one or more graphs).
*   **Select a query** to load it into the history editor.
*   **View per-query tabs**:
    *   Edit Query
    *   Profile
    *   Metadata
    *   Explain

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/browser/ui/query-history.html#deleting-history)
Deleting history
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The dialog supports:

*   Delete selected queries (supports multi-select via Ctrl + right-click)
*   Delete all queries

Persistence notes:

*   Stored under localStorage key: `query history`.

* * *

---

# Pro Tier | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/cloud/pro-tier.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

![FalkorDB Cloud Pro Tier Banner](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2d39df96-f932-4cba-a124-bfff93f9a0ca)

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cloud/pro-tier.html#pro-tier)
Pro Tier
===================================================================

FalkorDB’s **Pro Tier** is your solution for high-performance, production-ready graph database workloads, starting at **$350/Month**. This tier is designed for applications requiring **High Availability (HA)**, **Multi-zone Deployment**, and robust **Scalability**. It includes essential infrastructure features like **Cluster Deployment** and **Continuous Persistence (AOF + Snapshot)**, backed by **24-hour Dedicated Support**.

The Pro Tier provides a robust environment to scale your application with confidence. When your needs extend to features like VPC Peering, Advanced Monitoring, or a Dedicated Account Manager, you can easily upgrade to the Enterprise plan.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cloud/pro-tier.html#falkordb-pricing-plans-comparison)
FalkorDB Pricing Plans Comparison
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Feature | FREE | STARTUP | PRO | ENTERPRISE |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Monthly Cost (from)** | **Free** | **$73** | **$350** | **Custom** |
| Multi-Graph / Multi-Tenancy | ✓   | ✓   | **🟢** | ✓   |
| Graph Access Control | ✓   | ✓   | **🟢** | ✓   |
| **TLS** | ✗   | ✓   | **🟢** | ✓   |
| VPC | ✗   | ✗   | **🔴** | ✓   |
| Cluster Deployment | ✗   | ✗   | **🟢** | ✓   |
| High Availability | ✗   | ✗   | **🟢** | ✓   |
| Multi-zone Deployment | ✗   | ✗   | **🟢** | ✓   |
| Scalability | ✗   | ✗   | **🟢** | ✓   |
| Continuous Persistence | ✗   | ✗   | **🟢** | ✓   |
| **Automated Backups** | ✗   | Every 12 Hours | **Every 12 Hours** | Every Hour |
| Advanced Monitoring | ✗   | ✗   | **🔴** | ✓   |
| **Support** | Community | Community | **24/7** | Dedicated |
| Dedicated Account Manager | ✗   | ✗   | **🔴** | ✓   |
| **Cloud Providers** | AWS, GCP | AWS, GCP | **AWS, GCP** | AWS, GCP, Azure (BYOC) |
| **Get started** | [Sign up](https://app.falkordb.cloud/signup) | [Sign up](https://app.falkordb.cloud/signup) | [Sign up](https://app.falkordb.cloud/signup) | [Contact Us](mailto:info@falkordb.com) |

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cloud/pro-tier.html#terms)
Terms
-------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/cloud/pro-tier.html#pricing-calculation)
Pricing Calculation

> We charge deployments based on **Core/Hour** and **Memory GB/Hour** usage. You pay **$0.200 per Core/Hour** and **$0.01 per Memory GB/Hour**.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cloud/pro-tier.html#standalone)
Standalone
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

| Instance Type | Cores | Memory (GB) | Max Graph Dataset (GB) | Monthly Cost |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| E2-medium | 1   | 4   | 2   | $175.20 |
| t2.medium | 2   | 4   | 2   | $321.20 |
| E2-standard-2 / m6i.large (Starting Instance) | 2   | 8   | 6   | $350.40 |
| E2-standard-4 / m6i.xlarge | 4   | 16  | 12  | $700.80 |
| E2-standard-8 / m6i.2xlarge | 8   | 32  | 24  | $1,401.60 |
| E2-highmem-2 / r6i.large | 2   | 16  | 12  | $408.80 |
| E2-highmem-4 / r6i.xlarge | 4   | 32  | 24  | $817.60 |
| E2-highmem-8 / r6i.2xlarge | 8   | 64  | 48  | $1,635.20 |
| m6i.4xlarge | 16  | 64  | 48  | $2,803.20 |
| m6i.8xlarge | 32  | 128 | 96  | $5,606.40 |
| r6i.4xlarge | 16  | 128 | 96  | $3,270.40 |
| E2-custom-4-8192 / c6i.xlarge | 4   | 8   | 6   | $642.40 |
| E2-custom-8-16384 / c6i.2xlarge | 8   | 16  | 12  | $1,284.80 |
| E2-custom-16-32768 / c6i.4xlarge | 16  | 32  | 24  | $2,569.60 |
| E2-custom-32-65536 / c6i.8xlarge | 32  | 64  | 48  | $5,139.20 |

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cloud/pro-tier.html#replicated-high-availability-master-x1-replica-x1)
Replicated (High Availability, Master (x1), Replica (x1))
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Instances Type | Cores | Memory (GB) | Max Graph Dataset (GB) | Monthly Cost |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| E2-medium | 1   | 4   | 2   | $657.00 |
| t2.medium | 2   | 4   | 2   | $949.00 |
| E2-standard-2 / m6i.large (Starting Instance) | 2   | 8   | 6   | $1,007.40 |
| E2-standard-4 / m6i.xlarge | 4   | 16  | 12  | $1,708.20 |
| E2-standard-8 / m6i.2xlarge | 8   | 32  | 24  | $3,109.80 |
| E2-highmem-2 / r6i.large | 2   | 16  | 12  | $1,124.20 |
| E2-highmem-4 / r6i.xlarge | 4   | 32  | 24  | $1,941.80 |
| E2-highmem-8 / r6i.2xlarge | 8   | 64  | 48  | $3,577.00 |
| m6i.4xlarge | 16  | 64  | 48  | $5,913.00 |
| m6i.8xlarge | 32  | 128 | 96  | $11,519.40 |
| r6i.4xlarge | 16  | 128 | 96  | $6,847.40 |
| E2-custom-4-8192 / c6i.xlarge | 4   | 8   | 6   | $1,591.40 |
| E2-custom-8-16384 / c6i.2xlarge | 8   | 16  | 12  | $2,876.20 |

| E2-custom-32-65536 / c6i.8xlarge | 32  | 64  | 48  | $10,585.00 |

> Note: In the Replicated table, **Cores** and **Memory (GB)** are per instance. **Max Graph Dataset (GB)** is calculated as 2 GB for 4 GB containers and 75% of memory for containers larger than 4 GB. We charge an additional 2 cores and 2 GB for replication and cluster since they require an extra component (sentinel for replication and rebalancer for cluster).

> Use our **[graph size calculator](https://www.falkordb.com/graph-database-graph-size-calculator/)
> ** to further estimate your cost. ⚠️ Prices are subject to change
