# Mem0 | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#mem0)
Mem0
================================================================

[Mem0](https://github.com/mem0ai/mem0)
 is a memory management framework for AI agents that enables persistent, contextual memory across interactions. The [mem0-falkordb](https://github.com/FalkorDB/mem0-falkordb)
 plugin adds FalkorDB as a graph store backend for Mem0 without modifying any Mem0 source code.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#overview)
Overview
------------------------------------------------------------------------

The mem0-falkordb plugin provides:

*   **Graph-structured memory**: Store relationships between entities, not just flat facts
*   **Per-user graph isolation**: Each user gets their own isolated FalkorDB graph
*   **Context-aware retrieval**: Semantic search with vector embeddings
*   **Memory evolution**: Support for updates and conflict resolution
*   **Runtime patching**: No modifications to Mem0 source code required

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#why-mem0--falkordb)
Why Mem0 + FalkorDB?
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#falkordbs-added-value)
FalkorDB’s Added Value

*   **Native multi-graph support**: Isolated memory spaces for different users or agents
*   **Natural data isolation**: No user\_id filtering needed in Cypher queries
*   **Simpler, faster queries**: No WHERE clauses on user\_id
*   **Easy cleanup**: `delete_all` simply drops the user’s graph
*   **High performance**: Fast graph operations and efficient memory usage
*   **Cloud and on-premises ready**: Works with FalkorDB Cloud or your own deployment

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#use-cases)
Use Cases

*   **Multi-agent systems**: Persistent memory for each agent with graph-based relationships
*   **Conversational AI**: Track facts, entities, and relationships across conversations
*   **Personalized assistants**: Build context-aware AI that remembers user preferences and history
*   **Customer support**: Provide context-rich responses based on customer interaction history
*   **Knowledge management**: Aggregate and navigate complex information networks

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#getting-started)
Getting Started
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#prerequisites)
Prerequisites

*   Python 3.10 or higher
*   FalkorDB instance (Cloud or self-hosted)
*   OpenAI API key (or other supported LLM provider)

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#installation)
Installation

Install both Mem0 and the FalkorDB plugin:

    pip install mem0ai
    pip install mem0-falkordb
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#quick-start-example)
Quick Start Example

Here’s a complete example to get you started:

    from mem0_falkordb import register
    register()
    
    from mem0 import Memory
    
    config = {
        "graph_store": {
            "provider": "falkordb",
            "config": {
                "host": "localhost",
                "port": 6379,
                "database": "mem0",
            },
        },
        "llm": {
            "provider": "openai",
            "config": {"model": "gpt-4o-mini"},
        },
    }
    
    m = Memory.from_config(config)
    
    # Add memories for a user
    m.add("I love pizza", user_id="alice")
    m.add("Alice is a software engineer", user_id="alice")
    m.add("Alice works on AI projects", user_id="alice")
    
    # Search the memory
    results = m.search("what does alice like?", user_id="alice")
    print(results)
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#understanding-the-code)
Understanding the Code

1.  **Register the plugin**: Call `register()` to patch FalkorDB into Mem0’s factory system
2.  **Configure Mem0**: Set FalkorDB as the graph store provider with connection details
3.  **Add memories**: Store information with user\_id for automatic graph isolation
4.  **Search**: Query memories using natural language

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#running-falkordb)
Running FalkorDB
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#using-docker)
Using Docker

    docker run --rm -p 6379:6379 falkordb/falkordb:latest
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#using-falkordb-cloud)
Using FalkorDB Cloud

Sign up for a free account at [app.falkordb.cloud](https://app.falkordb.cloud/)
 and use the connection details in your config:

    config = {
        "graph_store": {
            "provider": "falkordb",
            "config": {
                "host": "your-instance.falkordb.cloud",
                "port": 6379,
                "username": "default",
                "password": "your-password",
                "database": "mem0",
            },
        },
        # ... rest of config
    }
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#configuration-options)
Configuration Options
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#graph-store-configuration)
Graph Store Configuration

    config = {
        "graph_store": {
            "provider": "falkordb",
            "config": {
                "host": "localhost",       # FalkorDB server host
                "port": 6379,              # FalkorDB server port
                "database": "mem0",        # Graph name prefix
                "username": None,          # Authentication username (optional)
                "password": None,          # Authentication password (optional)
                "base_label": True,        # Use __Entity__ base label
            },
        },
    }
    

| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `host` | str | `localhost` | FalkorDB server host |
| `port` | int | `6379` | FalkorDB server port |
| `database` | str | `mem0` | Graph name prefix (each user gets `{database}_{user_id}`) |
| `username` | str | `None` | Authentication username (optional) |
| `password` | str | `None` | Authentication password (optional) |
| `base_label` | bool | `True` | Use `__Entity__` base label |

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#per-user-graph-isolation)
Per-User Graph Isolation

Each user automatically gets their own isolated FalkorDB graph (e.g., `mem0_alice`, `mem0_bob`). This provides:

*   **Natural data isolation**: No user\_id filtering needed in Cypher queries
*   **Simpler, faster queries**: No WHERE clauses on user\_id
*   **Easy cleanup**: `delete_all` simply drops the user’s graph
*   **Scalability**: Leverage FalkorDB’s native multi-graph support

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#advanced-features)
Advanced Features
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#working-with-multiple-users)
Working with Multiple Users

    # Add memories for different users
    m.add("Alice loves pizza", user_id="alice")
    m.add("Bob prefers pasta", user_id="bob")
    
    # Each user gets their own isolated graph
    alice_results = m.search("what food?", user_id="alice")
    bob_results = m.search("what food?", user_id="bob")
    
    # Results are isolated per user
    print(alice_results)  # Returns info about pizza
    print(bob_results)    # Returns info about pasta
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#getting-all-memories)
Getting All Memories

    # Get all memories for a user
    all_memories = m.get_all(user_id="alice")
    for memory in all_memories:
        print(memory)
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#updating-memories)
Updating Memories

    # Mem0 automatically handles memory updates and conflict resolution
    m.add("Alice now prefers sushi", user_id="alice")
    results = m.search("what food does alice like?", user_id="alice")
    # Results will reflect the updated preference
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#deleting-memories)
Deleting Memories

    # Delete specific memories
    m.delete(memory_id="specific-id", user_id="alice")
    
    # Delete all memories for a user (drops the user's graph)
    m.delete_all(user_id="alice")
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#memory-history)
Memory History

    # Get memory history for a specific memory
    history = m.history(memory_id="specific-id")
    for entry in history:
        print(f"Version: {entry['version']}, Changed: {entry['changed_at']}")
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#demo)
Demo
----------------------------------------------------------------

The mem0-falkordb repository includes a comprehensive demo showcasing:

*   Graph-structured memory with relationships
*   Per-user graph isolation
*   Context-aware retrieval
*   Memory evolution and updates
*   Visual graph inspection

    # Clone the repository
    git clone https://github.com/FalkorDB/mem0-falkordb.git
    cd mem0-falkordb
    
    # Start FalkorDB
    docker run --rm -p 6379:6379 falkordb/falkordb:latest
    
    # Run the demo
    cd demo
    uv sync
    export OPENAI_API_KEY='your-key-here'
    uv run python demo.py
    

See the [demo README](https://github.com/FalkorDB/mem0-falkordb/blob/main/demo/README.md)
 for complete instructions.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#best-practices)
Best Practices
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1.  **Call register() once**: Always call `register()` before creating a Mem0 `Memory` instance
2.  **Use user\_id consistently**: Always provide user\_id for proper graph isolation
3.  **Configure LLM**: Ensure your LLM provider is properly configured for entity extraction
4.  **Monitor graph size**: Use `GRAPH.MEMORY USAGE` command to track memory usage
5.  **Clean up**: Use `delete_all()` to remove user graphs when no longer needed
6.  **Connection pooling**: Reuse Memory instances when possible

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#troubleshooting)
Troubleshooting
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#installation-issues)
Installation Issues

If you have trouble installing:

*   Ensure you have Python 3.10 or higher
*   Install Mem0 and mem0-falkordb separately
*   Try installing in a fresh virtual environment

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#connection-issues)
Connection Issues

If you can’t connect to FalkorDB:

*   Verify FalkorDB is running: `redis-cli -h localhost -p 6379 ping`
*   Check your connection details (host, port, credentials)
*   Ensure FalkorDB is accessible on the specified host and port

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#memory-not-being-stored)
Memory Not Being Stored

*   Make sure to call `register()` before creating the Memory instance
*   Verify your LLM API key is set correctly
*   Check that the user\_id is provided when adding memories
*   Ensure FalkorDB has enough memory available

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#search-returns-no-results)
Search Returns No Results

*   Verify memories were added successfully with `get_all()`
*   Check that you’re using the correct user\_id
*   Ensure your embeddings configuration is correct
*   Try more specific or different search queries

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#resources)
Resources
--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   📦 [PyPI Package](https://pypi.org/project/mem0-falkordb/)
    
*   💻 [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/FalkorDB/mem0-falkordb)

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/mem0.html#next-steps)
Next Steps
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Explore [Graphiti](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/graphiti.html)
     for temporal knowledge graph capabilities
*   Learn about [Cognee](https://docs.falkordb.com/agentic-memory/cognee.html)
     for alternative memory management approaches
*   Review [GenAI Tools](https://docs.falkordb.com/genai-tools)
     for graph reasoning and LLM integrations

# SET | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/set.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/set.html#set)
SET
=====================================================

The `SET` clause is used to create or update properties on nodes and relationships.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/set.html#setting-a-single-property)
Setting a Single Property
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

To set a property on a node:

    GRAPH.QUERY DEMO_GRAPH "MATCH (n { name: 'Jim' }) SET n.name = 'Bob'"
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/set.html#setting-multiple-properties)
Setting Multiple Properties
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can set multiple properties in a single `SET` clause by separating them with commas:

    GRAPH.QUERY DEMO_GRAPH
    "MATCH (n { name: 'Jim', age:32 })
    SET n.age = 33, n.name = 'Bob'"
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/set.html#setting-properties-from-a-map)
Setting Properties from a Map
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can set properties using a map. There are two operators with different behaviors:

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/set.html#replace-all-properties-)
Replace All Properties (`=`)

Replaces **all** existing properties with the map properties:

    GRAPH.QUERY DEMO_GRAPH
    "MATCH (n { name: 'Jim', age:32 })
    SET n = {age: 33, name: 'Bob'}"
    

**Result:** The node will have only the properties `age` and `name`. Any other existing properties are removed.

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/set.html#merge-properties-)
Merge Properties (`+=`)

Updates only the specified properties while keeping other existing properties:

    GRAPH.QUERY DEMO_GRAPH
    "MATCH (n { name: 'Jim', age:32 })
    SET n += {age: 33}"
    

**Result:** The node’s `age` is updated to 33, but `name` and any other properties remain unchanged.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/set.html#copying-properties-between-entities)
Copying Properties Between Entities
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can copy all properties from one entity to another:

    GRAPH.QUERY DEMO_GRAPH
    "MATCH (jim {name: 'Jim'}), (pam {name: 'Pam'})
    SET jim = pam"
    

After executing this query, the `jim` node will have exactly the same properties as the `pam` node (all of Jim’s original properties are replaced).

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/set.html#removing-properties)
Removing Properties
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

To remove a property, set its value to `NULL`:

    GRAPH.QUERY DEMO_GRAPH "MATCH (n { name: 'Jim' }) SET n.name = NULL"
    

This removes the `name` property from the node entirely.

* * *

---

# text.rpad | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/rpad.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/rpad.html#textrpad)
text.rpad
=========================================================================

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/rpad.html#description)
Description
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Pads the end (right side) of a string with a specified character until it reaches the desired length.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/rpad.html#syntax)
Syntax
--------------------------------------------------------------------

    flex.text.rpad(string, length, padChar)
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/rpad.html#parameters)
Parameters
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `string` | string/number | Yes | The string to pad (will be converted to string if number) |
| `length` | number | Yes | The target length after padding |
| `padChar` | string | No  | The character to use for padding (default: space ‘ ‘) |

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/rpad.html#returns)
Returns
----------------------------------------------------------------------

**Type:** string

The padded string. If the original string is already longer than the target length, it is returned unchanged. Returns `null` if input is `null`.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/rpad.html#examples)
Examples
------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/rpad.html#example-1-basic-right-padding)
Example 1: Basic Right Padding

    RETURN flex.text.rpad('test', 8, '-') AS result
    

**Output:**

    result
    ----------
    test----
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/rpad.html#example-2-creating-fixed-width-fields)
Example 2: Creating Fixed-Width Fields

    MATCH (p:Product)
    RETURN flex.text.rpad(p.name, 20, ' ') AS productName, p.price
    

**Output:**

    productName          | price
    ---------------------|-------
    Laptop               | 999.99
    Mouse                | 29.99
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/rpad.html#example-3-building-formatted-tables)
Example 3: Building Formatted Tables

    WITH [['Name', 15], ['Email', 25], ['Role', 10]] AS columns
    UNWIND columns AS col
    RETURN flex.text.rpad(col[0], col[1], ' ') AS header
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/rpad.html#notes)
Notes
------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Returns `null` if input is `null`
*   Converts numbers to strings automatically
*   Default padding character is a space
*   If string is already longer than target length, returns original string
*   Useful for creating aligned columns, fixed-width output, or formatted tables

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/rpad.html#see-also)
See Also
------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   [text.lpad](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/lpad.html)
     - Pad the start (left side) of a string
*   [text.repeat](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/repeat.html)
     - Repeat a string multiple times
*   [text.format](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/format.html)
     - Format strings with placeholders

* * *

# RETURN | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/return.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/return.html#return)
RETURN
==============================================================

The `RETURN` clause defines which properties and values the result-set will contain.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/return.html#basic-usage)
Basic Usage
------------------------------------------------------------------------

The basic structure is a comma-separated list of `alias.property` expressions:

    RETURN person.name, person.age
    

For convenience, you can specify just the alias to return all properties of an entity:

    RETURN movie.title, actor
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/return.html#removing-duplicates)
Removing Duplicates
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Use the `DISTINCT` keyword to remove duplicate values from the result-set:

    RETURN DISTINCT friend_of_friend.name
    

For example, if you have two friends (Joe and Miesha) who both know Dominick, `DISTINCT` ensures that Dominick appears only once in the final result set.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/return.html#aggregations)
Aggregations
--------------------------------------------------------------------------

The `RETURN` clause can also aggregate data, similar to SQL’s GROUP BY functionality.

When an aggregation function is used in the RETURN list, all non-aggregated values become implicit grouping keys:

    RETURN movie.title, MAX(actor.age), MIN(actor.age)
    

This query groups data by movie title and, for each movie, returns the youngest and oldest actor ages.

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/return.html#supported-aggregation-functions)
Supported Aggregation Functions

| Function | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `avg` | Calculate average of numeric values |
| `collect` | Collect values into a list |
| `count` | Count number of values |
| `max` | Find maximum value |
| `min` | Find minimum value |
| `percentileCont` | Calculate continuous percentile |
| `percentileDisc` | Calculate discrete percentile |
| `stDev` | Calculate standard deviation |
| `sum` | Calculate sum of numeric values |

For detailed information on aggregation functions, see the [Functions documentation](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/functions#aggregating-functions)
.

* * *

---

# SKIP | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/skip.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher/skip.html#skip)
SKIP
========================================================

The optional skip clause allows a specified number of records to be omitted from the result set.

    SKIP <number of records to skip>
    

This can be useful when processing results in batches. A query that would examine the second 100-element batch of nodes with the label `Person`, for example, would be:

    GRAPH.QUERY DEMO_GRAPH "MATCH (p:Person) RETURN p ORDER BY p.name SKIP 100 LIMIT 100"
    

* * *

---

# Settings Page | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/browser/ui/settings.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/browser/ui/settings.html#settings-page)
Settings Page
==================================================================================

The Settings page (`/settings`) is where you configure Browser behavior and manage security/admin features.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/browser/ui/settings.html#tabs--sections)
Tabs / sections
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Settings page is organized into these sections:

*   **Browser Settings** (all users)
*   **Personal Access Tokens** (all users)
*   **DB Configurations** (Admin only; requires online connection)
*   **Users** (Admin only; requires online connection)

If you navigate away from **Browser Settings** while there are unsaved changes, the UI prompts you to **Save** or **Discard** changes.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/browser/ui/settings.html#browser-settings)
Browser Settings
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Browser Settings panel is a page that can be scrolled, with grouped sections (collapsed/expanded in the UI). Common settings include:

*   **Query execution**
    *   Query timeout
    *   Result limits
    *   Default query behavior (run a default query when selecting a graph)
*   **User experience**
    *   Content persistence (restores the last used graph + query from localStorage)
    *   Node caption priority keys (controls how node labels/captions are picked)
    *   Property-key display options (e.g. prefix behavior)
*   **Graph Info**
    *   Refresh interval (how often graph info refreshes)
*   **Chat**
    *   LLM provider API key
    *   Model selection
    *   How many interactions to store locally

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/browser/ui/settings.html#replay-tutorial)
Replay Tutorial

Browser Settings includes a **Replay Tutorial** action, which re-runs the guided tour overlay.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/browser/ui/settings.html#personal-access-tokens)
Personal Access Tokens
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Tokens section provides a UI to:

*   Create tokens (often with optional expiry)
*   View/revoke existing tokens

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/browser/ui/settings.html#admin-only-sections)
Admin-only sections
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/browser/ui/settings.html#db-configurations-admin)
DB Configurations (Admin)

Allows viewing/updating database configuration values via the server API.

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/browser/ui/settings.html#users-admin)
Users (Admin)

Allows:

*   Viewing users
*   Changing roles
*   Adding/removing users

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/browser/ui/settings.html#keyboard-shortcut)
Keyboard shortcut
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   `Esc` navigates back (with a save/discard prompt if there are unsaved Browser Settings changes).

* * *

# bitwise.shiftLeft | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftLeft.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftLeft.html#bitwiseshiftleft)
bitwise.shiftLeft
=================================================================================================

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftLeft.html#description)
Description
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Performs a left bit shift operation, moving all bits to the left by the specified number of positions. Zero bits are shifted in from the right.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftLeft.html#syntax)
Syntax
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

    flex.bitwise.shiftLeft(a, positions)
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftLeft.html#parameters)
Parameters
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `a` | number (integer) | Yes | The value to shift |
| `positions` | number (integer) | Yes | Number of positions to shift left |

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftLeft.html#returns)
Returns
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Type:** number (integer)

The result of shifting the bits left by the specified positions.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftLeft.html#examples)
Examples
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftLeft.html#example-1-basic-left-shift)
Example 1: Basic Left Shift

    RETURN flex.bitwise.shiftLeft(5, 2) AS result
    

**Output:**

    result
    ------
    20
    

(Binary: 0101 « 2 = 10100 = 20)

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftLeft.html#example-2-multiply-by-power-of-two)
Example 2: Multiply by Power of Two

    WITH 7 AS value
    RETURN 
        flex.bitwise.shiftLeft(value, 1) AS times2,
        flex.bitwise.shiftLeft(value, 2) AS times4,
        flex.bitwise.shiftLeft(value, 3) AS times8
    

**Output:**

    times2 | times4 | times8
    -------|--------|-------
    14     | 28     | 56
    

(Left shift by n is equivalent to multiplying by 2^n)

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftLeft.html#example-3-creating-bit-masks)
Example 3: Creating Bit Masks

    RETURN flex.bitwise.shiftLeft(1, 3) AS mask
    

**Output:**

    mask
    ----
    8
    

(Creates mask with bit 3 set: 1000)

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftLeft.html#notes)
Notes
--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Operates on 32-bit signed integers in JavaScript
*   Left shift by n is equivalent to multiplying by 2^n
*   Bits shifted off the left are discarded
*   Zero bits are shifted in from the right
*   Useful for multiplication by powers of 2 and creating bit masks

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftLeft.html#see-also)
See Also
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   [bitwise.shiftRight](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftRight.html)
     - Shift bits to the right
*   [bitwise.and](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/and.html)
     - Bitwise AND operation
*   [bitwise.or](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/or.html)
     - Bitwise OR operation

* * *

---

# bitwise.shiftRight | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftRight.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftRight.html#bitwiseshiftright)
bitwise.shiftRight
====================================================================================================

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftRight.html#description)
Description
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Performs a sign-propagating right bit shift operation, moving all bits to the right by the specified number of positions. The sign bit is copied to fill the leftmost positions.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftRight.html#syntax)
Syntax
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

    flex.bitwise.shiftRight(a, positions)
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftRight.html#parameters)
Parameters
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `a` | number (integer) | Yes | The value to shift |
| `positions` | number (integer) | Yes | Number of positions to shift right |

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftRight.html#returns)
Returns
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Type:** number (integer)

The result of shifting the bits right by the specified positions, with sign extension.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftRight.html#examples)
Examples
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftRight.html#example-1-basic-right-shift)
Example 1: Basic Right Shift

    RETURN flex.bitwise.shiftRight(20, 2) AS result
    

**Output:**

    result
    ------
    5
    

(Binary: 10100 » 2 = 00101 = 5)

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftRight.html#example-2-divide-by-power-of-two)
Example 2: Divide by Power of Two

    WITH 56 AS value
    RETURN 
        flex.bitwise.shiftRight(value, 1) AS div2,
        flex.bitwise.shiftRight(value, 2) AS div4,
        flex.bitwise.shiftRight(value, 3) AS div8
    

**Output:**

    div2 | div4 | div8
    -----|------|-----
    28   | 14   | 7
    

(Right shift by n is equivalent to integer division by 2^n)

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftRight.html#example-3-sign-extension-with-negative-numbers)
Example 3: Sign Extension with Negative Numbers

    RETURN flex.bitwise.shiftRight(-8, 2) AS result
    

**Output:**

    result
    ------
    -2
    

(Sign bit is preserved in right shift)

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftRight.html#example-4-extracting-higher-bits)
Example 4: Extracting Higher Bits

    WITH 255 AS value  // 11111111
    RETURN flex.bitwise.shiftRight(value, 4) AS upperNibble
    

**Output:**

    upperNibble
    -----------
    15
    

(Extracts upper 4 bits: 00001111 = 15)

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftRight.html#notes)
Notes
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Operates on 32-bit signed integers in JavaScript
*   Uses arithmetic (sign-propagating) right shift
*   Right shift by n is equivalent to integer division by 2^n (truncated toward negative infinity)
*   Sign bit is copied to fill vacated positions (sign extension)
*   Bits shifted off the right are discarded
*   Useful for division by powers of 2 and extracting bit fields

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftRight.html#see-also)
See Also
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   [bitwise.shiftLeft](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/shiftLeft.html)
     - Shift bits to the left
*   [bitwise.and](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/and.html)
     - Bitwise AND operation
*   [bitwise.or](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/bitwise/or.html)
     - Bitwise OR operation

* * *

---

# coll.shuffle | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/collections/shuffle.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/collections/shuffle.html#collshuffle)
coll.shuffle
=========================================================================================

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/collections/shuffle.html#description)
Description
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Randomly shuffles the elements of a list using the Fisher-Yates algorithm. Returns a new list with elements in random order without modifying the original.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/collections/shuffle.html#syntax)
Syntax
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    flex.coll.shuffle(list)
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/collections/shuffle.html#parameters)
Parameters
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `list` | list | Yes | The list to shuffle |

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/collections/shuffle.html#returns)
Returns
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Type:** list

A new list containing the same elements in a randomized order. Returns an empty list if input is not an array.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/collections/shuffle.html#examples)
Examples
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/collections/shuffle.html#example-1-basic-shuffle)
Example 1: Basic Shuffle

    WITH [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] AS numbers
    RETURN flex.coll.shuffle(numbers) AS shuffled
    

**Output:** (example, actual order will vary)

    shuffled
    -----------
    [3, 1, 5, 2, 4]
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/collections/shuffle.html#example-2-random-sample-selection)
Example 2: Random Sample Selection

    MATCH (q:Question)
    WITH collect(q) AS allQuestions
    WITH flex.coll.shuffle(allQuestions) AS randomized
    RETURN randomized[0..10] AS quizQuestions
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/collections/shuffle.html#example-3-randomizing-recommendations)
Example 3: Randomizing Recommendations

    MATCH (u:User {id: $userId})-[:LIKES]->(p:Product)
    MATCH (p)-[:SIMILAR_TO]->(rec:Product)
    WITH collect(DISTINCT rec) AS recommendations
    RETURN flex.coll.shuffle(recommendations)[0..5] AS randomRecs
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/collections/shuffle.html#example-4-random-team-assignment)
Example 4: Random Team Assignment

    MATCH (p:Player)
    WITH collect(p.name) AS players
    WITH flex.coll.shuffle(players) AS shuffled
    RETURN shuffled[0..5] AS team1, shuffled[5..10] AS team2
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/collections/shuffle.html#notes)
Notes
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Returns empty list if input is not an array or is `null`
*   Uses the Fisher-Yates shuffle algorithm for uniform random distribution
*   Creates a new list; does not modify the original
*   Each element appears exactly once in the result
*   Order is truly random on each execution

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/collections/shuffle.html#see-also)
See Also
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   [coll.zip](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/collections/zip.html)
     - Combine two lists element-by-element
*   [coll.union](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/collections/union.html)
     - Combine unique elements from lists

* * *

---

# text.snakeCase | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/snakeCase.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/snakeCase.html#textsnakecase)
text.snakeCase
========================================================================================

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/snakeCase.html#description)
Description
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Converts a string to snake\_case format by separating words with underscores and converting all characters to lowercase.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/snakeCase.html#syntax)
Syntax
-------------------------------------------------------------------------

    flex.text.snakeCase(string)
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/snakeCase.html#parameters)
Parameters
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `string` | string | Yes | The string to convert to snake\_case |

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/snakeCase.html#returns)
Returns
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Type:** string

The input string converted to snake\_case format. Returns the original string if no matches are found. Returns `null` if input is `null`.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/snakeCase.html#examples)
Examples
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/snakeCase.html#example-1-basic-usage)
Example 1: Basic Usage

    RETURN flex.text.snakeCase('HelloWorld') AS result
    

**Output:**

    result
    -----------
    hello_world
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/snakeCase.html#example-2-converting-multiple-formats)
Example 2: Converting Multiple Formats

    WITH ['camelCase', 'PascalCase', 'kebab-case', 'space separated'] AS inputs
    UNWIND inputs AS input
    RETURN input AS original, flex.text.snakeCase(input) AS snake
    

**Output:**

    original        | snake
    ----------------|------------------
    camelCase       | camel_case
    PascalCase      | pascal_case
    kebab-case      | kebab_case
    space separated | space_separated
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/snakeCase.html#example-3-database-column-naming)
Example 3: Database Column Naming

    MATCH (u:User)
    WITH u, keys(u) AS properties
    UNWIND properties AS prop
    RETURN prop AS camelCase, flex.text.snakeCase(prop) AS dbColumn
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/snakeCase.html#notes)
Notes
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Returns `null` for `null` input
*   Handles multiple word boundary detection patterns
*   All characters are converted to lowercase
*   Words are separated by underscores
*   Common for database column names and Python conventions
*   Returns the original string if no word boundaries are detected

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/snakeCase.html#see-also)
See Also
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   [text.camelCase](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/camelCase.html)
     - Convert to camelCase format
*   [text.upperCamelCase](https://docs.falkordb.com/udfs/flex/text/upperCamelCase.html)
     - Convert to UpperCamelCase format

* * *

---

# Kuzu to FalkorDB | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#kuzu-to-falkordb-migration)
Kuzu to FalkorDB Migration
==============================================================================================================================

A streamlined 2-step process to migrate data from Kuzu graph database into FalkorDB using automated schema discovery and CSV export.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#overview)
Overview
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This migration tool bridges the gap between Kuzu and FalkorDB by:

1.  Automatically discovering your Kuzu database schema
2.  Exporting all nodes and relationships to properly formatted CSV files
3.  Loading these CSV files into FalkorDB using the FalkorDB Rust loader

The process ensures complete data migration including nodes, relationships, properties, and metadata.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#features)
Features
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Automatic Schema Discovery**: Dynamically discovers all node types and relationship types in your Kuzu database
*   **FalkorDB Compatibility**: Generates CSV files in the exact format expected by FalkorDB
*   **Intelligent Label Mapping**: Maps Kuzu relationship names to standardized FalkorDB edge types
*   **Complex Property Handling**: Properly handles lists, nested values, and various data types
*   **Comprehensive Export**: Exports both nodes and relationships with full metadata
*   **Schema Documentation**: Optional JSON schema file generation for documentation purposes

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#prerequisites)
Prerequisites
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Python 3.9+
*   `kuzu` Python package
*   FalkorDB instance (local, Docker, or Cloud)
*   [FalkorDB Rust Loader](https://github.com/FalkorDB/FalkorDB-Loader-RS)
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#installation)
Installation
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1.  Install the required dependencies:

    pip3 install kuzu
    

1.  Download the migration script:

    git clone https://github.com/FalkorDB/Kuzu-to-FalkorDB.git
    cd Kuzu-to-FalkorDB
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#step-1-exporting-from-kuzu)
Step 1: Exporting from Kuzu
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#basic-usage)
Basic Usage

Export all data from a Kuzu database:

    python3 kuzu_to_falkordb_export.py --db path/to/your/database
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#advanced-usage)
Advanced Usage

    # Export with schema documentation
    python3 kuzu_to_falkordb_export.py --db network_it_smart_db --schema schema.json
    
    # Export to custom directory
    python3 kuzu_to_falkordb_export.py --db network_it_smart_db --output my_csv_export
    
    # Full example with all options
    python3 kuzu_to_falkordb_export.py --db network_it_smart_db --schema schema.json --output falkordb_import
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#command-line-options)
Command Line Options

| Option | Description | Required | Default |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `--db`, `--database` | Path to Kuzu database file/directory | Yes | \-  |
| `--schema` | Path to output schema JSON file | No  | None |
| `--output` | Output directory for CSV files | No  | `csv_output` |

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#output-structure)
Output Structure

The export script generates the following files:

**Node CSV Files:**

*   Format: `nodes_<NodeType>.csv`
*   Structure: `id,labels,property1,property2,...`
*   Example: `nodes_Application.csv`, `nodes_Machine.csv`

**Edge CSV Files:**

*   Format: `edges_<EdgeType>.csv`
*   Structure: `source,source_label,target,target_label,type`
*   Example: `edges_CONNECTS.csv`, `edges_CONTAINS.csv`

**Schema File (Optional):**

*   File: `schema.json`
*   Contains: Export metadata, node types, relationship types, and file mappings

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#example-export-output)
Example Export Output

    🚀 Kuzu to FalkorDB CSV Exporter
    ==================================================
    Database: network_it_smart_db
    Output: _csv_
    Schema: schema.json
    ==================================================
    
    🔍 Discovering database schema...
      📦 Found node table: Application
      📦 Found node table: Machine
      🔗 Found relationship table: CONNECTS -> CONNECTS
      🔗 Found relationship table: INSTANCE_APP_SW -> INSTANCE
      ✓ Discovered 8 node types and 15 relationship types
    
    📤 Exporting 8 node types...
      📦 Exporting Application...
        ✓ Exported 1,234 Application nodes to nodes_Application.csv
    
    🔗 Exporting 15 relationship types...
      🔗 Exporting CONNECTS (from 3 Kuzu tables)...
        ✓ Exported 5,678 CONNECTS relationships to edges_CONNECTS.csv
    
    🎉 Export completed successfully!
    📁 Output files in: _csv_
    📊 Exported: 8 node types, 15 relationship types
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#step-2-loading-into-falkordb)
Step 2: Loading into FalkorDB
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Use the high-performance [FalkorDB Rust Loader](https://github.com/FalkorDB/FalkorDB-Loader-RS)
 to load the exported CSV files directly into FalkorDB.

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#installation-1)
Installation

    git clone https://github.com/FalkorDB/FalkorDB-Loader-RS
    cd FalkorDB-Loader-RS
    cargo build --release
    

The binary will be available at `target/release/falkordb-loader`.

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#basic-usage-1)
Basic Usage

After exporting your Kuzu database to CSV files, load them into FalkorDB:

    ./target/release/falkordb-loader my_graph
    

This command will:

1.  Connect to FalkorDB (localhost:6379 by default)
2.  Create the graph `my_graph`
3.  Load all CSV files from the `csv_output` directory
4.  Create indexes and constraints automatically

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#advanced-usage-1)
Advanced Usage

For more control over the loading process:

    ./target/release/falkordb-loader my_graph \
      --host localhost \
      --port 6379 \
      --username myuser \
      --password mypass \
      --csv-dir ./_csv_ \
      --batch-size 1000 \
      --merge-mode \
      --stats \
      --progress-interval 500
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#command-line-options-1)
Command-Line Options

| Option | Description | Default |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `graph_name` | Target graph name in FalkorDB (required) | \-  |
| `--host` | FalkorDB host | localhost |
| `--port` | FalkorDB port | 6379 |
| `--username` | FalkorDB username (optional) | \-  |
| `--password` | FalkorDB password (optional) | \-  |
| `--csv-dir` | Directory containing CSV files | csv\_output |
| `--batch-size` | Batch size for loading | 5000 |
| `--merge-mode` | Use MERGE instead of CREATE for upsert | false |
| `--stats` | Show graph statistics after loading | false |
| `--progress-interval` | Report progress every N records (0 to disable) | 1000 |

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#performance-features)
Performance Features

The Rust loader provides significant advantages for loading Kuzu exports:

*   **Async Operations**: All database operations use async/await for better concurrency
*   **Batch Processing**: Processes multiple records per query (default: 5000)
*   **Memory Efficient**: Streams data from CSV files without loading everything into memory
*   **Progress Tracking**: Real-time progress updates during loading
*   **Error Handling**: Comprehensive error handling with detailed logging

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#example-output)
Example Output

    [INFO] Loading graph: my_graph
    [INFO] CSV directory: _csv_
    [INFO] Batch size: 5000
    [INFO] Found 8 node files and 15 edge files
    
    [INFO] Creating indexes...
    [INFO] Creating constraints...
    
    [INFO] Loading nodes...
    [INFO] Loading nodes from nodes_Application.csv...
    [INFO] Progress: 1000/1234 nodes loaded
    [INFO] ✓ Loaded 1234 Application nodes
    
    [INFO] Loading edges...
    [INFO] Loading edges from edges_CONNECTS.csv...
    [INFO] Progress: 5000/5678 edges loaded
    [INFO] ✓ Loaded 5678 CONNECTS relationships
    
    [INFO] Loading complete!
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#performance-tips)
Performance Tips

1.  **Match export and loader directories**: If you used `--output my_csv_export` during export, use `--csv-dir my_csv_export` when loading
2.  **Adjust batch size**: For very large datasets, you might want to increase batch size: `--batch-size 10000`
3.  **Monitor progress**: Use `--progress-interval` to get regular updates
4.  **Enable verbose logging**: Set `RUST_LOG=debug` for detailed information
5.  **Use stats**: Add `--stats` to see a summary of loaded data after completion

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#data-mapping-features)
Data Mapping Features
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#relationship-mapping)
Relationship Mapping

The script intelligently maps Kuzu relationship names to standardized FalkorDB edge types, ensuring consistent naming conventions.

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#label-enhancement)
Label Enhancement

The script enhances node labels with context for better FalkorDB compatibility:

*   Process nodes in different contexts: `Application:Process`, `Service:Process`, or `OS:Process`
*   Network zones: `Network:Zone`
*   Service software: `Software:Service`

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#error-handling)
Error Handling
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The migration script includes robust error handling:

*   Validates database path exists
*   Handles missing relationship types gracefully
*   Continues export even if individual tables fail
*   Provides detailed progress and error messages

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#troubleshooting)
Troubleshooting
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#common-issues)
Common Issues

1.  **Database not found**: Ensure the database path is correct and accessible
2.  **Permission errors**: Check write permissions for the output directory
3.  **Memory issues**: For very large databases, consider adjusting batch sizes or processing in chunks

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#debug-mode)
Debug Mode

For additional debugging information, you can modify the script to include more verbose logging or add print statements to track the export process.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#additional-resources)
Additional Resources
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   [Kuzu-to-FalkorDB GitHub Repository](https://github.com/FalkorDB/Kuzu-to-FalkorDB)
    
*   [FalkorDB Rust Loader](https://github.com/FalkorDB/FalkorDB-Loader-RS)
    
*   [FalkorDB Bulk Loader](https://github.com/falkordb/falkordb-bulk-loader)
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/migration/kuzu-to-falkordb.html#next-steps)
Next Steps
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Explore [FalkorDB Cypher Language](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher)
     for querying your graph
*   Learn about [FalkorDB Operations](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations)
     for production deployments
*   Check out [FalkorDB Integration](https://docs.falkordb.com/integration)
     options

* * *

# FalkorDBLite (Python) | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/falkordblite/falkordblite-py.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/falkordblite/falkordblite-py.html#falkordblite-for-python)
FalkorDBLite for Python
==========================================================================================================================

FalkorDBLite is a self-contained Python interface to the FalkorDB graph database. It provides an embedded Redis server with the FalkorDB module that is automatically installed, configured, and managed when the bindings are used.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/falkordblite/falkordblite-py.html#key-features)
Key Features
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Easy to Use** - Built-in Redis server with FalkorDB module that is automatically installed, configured, and managed
*   **Graph Database** - Full support for FalkorDB graph operations using Cypher queries through a simple Python API
*   **Flexible** - Create a single server shared by multiple programs or multiple independent servers with graph capabilities
*   **Compatible** - Provides both Redis key-value operations and FalkorDB graph operations in a unified interface
*   **Secure** - Uses a secure default Redis configuration that is only accessible by the creating user on the local system
*   **Persistent** - Data persists between sessions when using the same database file

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/falkordblite/falkordblite-py.html#requirements)
Requirements
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Python 3.12 or higher

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/falkordblite/falkordblite-py.html#installation)
Installation
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Install FalkorDBLite using pip:

    pip install falkordblite
    

The package will automatically install its dependencies, including:

*   `redis>=4.5` - Redis Python client
*   `psutil` - Process and system utilities

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/falkordblite/falkordblite-py.html#macos-runtime-requirement)
macOS Runtime Requirement

**Important:** On macOS, the FalkorDB module requires the OpenMP runtime library (`libomp`). If you encounter an error like `Library not loaded: /opt/homebrew/opt/libomp/lib/libomp.dylib`, install it using Homebrew:

    brew install libomp
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/falkordblite/falkordblite-py.html#getting-started)
Getting Started
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FalkorDBLite provides two main interfaces:

1.  **FalkorDB Graph API** - A graph database interface using Cypher queries
2.  **Redis API** - Traditional Redis key-value operations

The package includes both Redis and the FalkorDB module, automatically configured and managed.

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/falkordblite/falkordblite-py.html#basic-graph-database-usage)
Basic Graph Database Usage

Here’s a simple example of creating a graph database, adding nodes and relationships, and querying them using Cypher:

    from redislite.falkordb_client import FalkorDB
    
    # Create a FalkorDB instance with embedded Redis + FalkorDB
    db = FalkorDB('/tmp/falkordb.db')
    
    # Select a graph
    g = db.select_graph('social')
    
    # Create nodes with Cypher
    result = g.query('CREATE (p:Person {name: "Alice", age: 30}) RETURN p')
    result = g.query('CREATE (p:Person {name: "Bob", age: 25}) RETURN p')
    
    # Create a relationship
    result = g.query('''
        MATCH (a:Person {name: "Alice"}), (b:Person {name: "Bob"})
        CREATE (a)-[r:KNOWS]->(b)
        RETURN r
    ''')
    
    # Query the graph
    result = g.query('MATCH (p:Person) RETURN p.name, p.age')
    for row in result.result_set:
        print(row)
    
    # Read-only query
    result = g.ro_query('MATCH (p:Person)-[r:KNOWS]->(f) RETURN p.name, f.name')
    for row in result.result_set:
        print(f"{row[0]} knows {row[1]}")
    
    # Delete the graph when done
    g.delete()
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/falkordblite/falkordblite-py.html#complex-graph-example)
Complex Graph Example

Here’s a more complex example demonstrating graph relationships:

    from redislite.falkordb_client import FalkorDB
    
    db = FalkorDB('/tmp/graphs.db')
    g = db.select_graph('social')
    
    # Create a graph with multiple nodes and relationships
    g.query('''
        CREATE (alice:Person {name: "Alice", age: 30}),
               (bob:Person {name: "Bob", age: 25}),
               (carol:Person {name: "Carol", age: 28}),
               (alice)-[:KNOWS]->(bob),
               (bob)-[:KNOWS]->(carol),
               (alice)-[:KNOWS]->(carol)
    ''')
    
    # Find all friends of Alice
    result = g.query('''
        MATCH (p:Person {name: "Alice"})-[:KNOWS]->(friend)
        RETURN friend.name, friend.age
    ''')
    
    for row in result.result_set:
        print(f"Friend: {row[0]}, Age: {row[1]}")
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/falkordblite/falkordblite-py.html#using-redis-key-value-operations)
Using Redis Key-Value Operations

FalkorDBLite also supports traditional Redis operations:

    from redislite import Redis
    
    # Create a Redis connection
    redis_connection = Redis('/tmp/redis.db')
    
    # Use standard Redis operations
    redis_connection.set('key', 'value')
    value = redis_connection.get('key')
    print(value)  # b'value'
    
    # List all keys
    keys = redis_connection.keys()
    print(keys)
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/falkordblite/falkordblite-py.html#working-with-multiple-graphs)
Working with Multiple Graphs

You can work with multiple independent graphs in the same database:

    from redislite.falkordb_client import FalkorDB
    
    db = FalkorDB('/tmp/multi.db')
    
    # Create different graphs for different domains
    users = db.select_graph('users')
    products = db.select_graph('products')
    transactions = db.select_graph('transactions')
    
    # Each graph is independent
    users.query('CREATE (u:User {name: "Alice"})')
    products.query('CREATE (p:Product {name: "Laptop"})')
    
    # List all graphs
    all_graphs = db.list_graphs()
    print(all_graphs)
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/falkordblite/falkordblite-py.html#data-persistence)
Data Persistence
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FalkorDB data persists between sessions when you use the same database file:

    from redislite.falkordb_client import FalkorDB
    
    # First session - create data
    db = FalkorDB('/tmp/falkordb.db')
    g = db.select_graph('social')
    g.query('CREATE (p:Person {name: "Alice", age: 30})')
    
    # Close and reopen
    db = FalkorDB('/tmp/falkordb.db')
    g = db.select_graph('social')
    
    # Data from previous session is still there
    result = g.query('MATCH (p:Person) RETURN p.name, p.age')
    for row in result.result_set:
        print(f"Name: {row[0]}, Age: {row[1]}")
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/falkordblite/falkordblite-py.html#use-cases)
Use Cases
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

FalkorDBLite is ideal for:

*   **Development and Testing** - Quickly test graph database functionality without setting up a full server
*   **Prototyping** - Rapidly prototype graph-based applications
*   **Educational Purposes** - Learn graph databases and Cypher queries without infrastructure setup
*   **Small-Scale Applications** - Lightweight applications that need graph database capabilities
*   **CI/CD Pipelines** - Automated testing environments that require an embedded database

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/falkordblite/falkordblite-py.html#production)
Production
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

For production environments, consider using:

*   [FalkorDB Cloud](https://app.falkordb.cloud/)
     - Managed cloud service
*   [FalkorDB Docker](https://docs.falkordb.com/getting-started)
     - Self-hosted server deployment
*   [FalkorDB Kubernetes](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/k8s_support)
     - Kubernetes deployment

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/falkordblite/falkordblite-py.html#additional-resources)
Additional Resources
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   [FalkorDBLite GitHub Repository](https://github.com/FalkorDB/falkordblite)
    
*   [FalkorDBLite on PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/falkordblite/)
    
*   [FalkorDB Documentation](https://docs.falkordb.com/index)
    
*   [FalkorDB Cypher Language](https://docs.falkordb.com/cypher)
    
*   [FalkorDB Python Client](https://github.com/FalkorDB/falkordb-py)
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/falkordblite/falkordblite-py.html#getting-help)
Getting Help
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you encounter issues with FalkorDBLite:

1.  Check the [FalkorDBLite GitHub Issues](https://github.com/FalkorDB/falkordblite/issues)
    
2.  Review the [troubleshooting guide](https://github.com/FalkorDB/falkordblite/blob/master/TROUBLESHOOTING.md)

# Replication | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/replication.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/replication.html#configuring-falkordb-docker-for-replication)
Configuring FalkorDB Docker for Replication
=================================================================================================================================================

FalkorDB supports advanced configurations to enable replication, ensuring that your data is available and synchronized across multiple instances. This guide will walk you through setting up FalkorDB in Docker with replication enabled, providing high availability and data redundancy.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/replication.html#prerequisites)
Prerequisites
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Before you begin, ensure you have the following:

*   Docker installed on your machine.
*   A working FalkorDB Docker image. You can pull it from Docker Hub.
*   Basic knowledge of Docker commands and configurations.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/replication.html#step-1-configuring-replication)
Step 1: Configuring Replication
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Replication ensures that your data is available across multiple FalkorDB instances. You configure one instance as the primary (master) and others as replicas.

To enable communication between FalkorDB containers, we first need to set up a Docker network.

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/replication.html#11-creating-a-network)
1.1 Creating a Network

First, create a Docker network to allow communication between the FalkorDB nodes.

    docker network create falkordb-network
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/replication.html#11-setting-up-the-master-instance)
1.1 Setting up the Master Instance

Start the master FalkorDB instance:

    docker run -d \
      --name falkordb-master \
      -v falkordb_data:/data \
      -p 6379:6379 \
      --network falkordb-network \
      falkordb/falkordb
    

This instance runs in standalone mode and will serve as the primary (master) node.

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/replication.html#12-setting-up-the-replica-instance)
1.2 Setting up the Replica Instance

Next, start the replica instance:

    docker run -d \
      --name falkordb-replica1 \
      -p 6380:6379 \
      --network falkordb-network \
      falkordb/falkordb
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/replication.html#13-configuring-replication)
1.3 Configuring Replication

Connect to the replica instance and configure it to replicate data from the master:

    docker exec -it falkordb-replica1 /bin/bash 
    redis-cli replicaof falkordb-master 6379
    

This command tells the replica to replicate data from the master instance.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/replication.html#step-2-verifying-the-setup)
Step 2: Verifying the Setup
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

To verify that replication is working correctly:

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/replication.html#21-insert-data-on-master)
2.1 Insert Data on Master

    # Connect to the master
    docker exec -it falkordb-master redis-cli
    GRAPH.QUERY mygraph "CREATE (:Database {name:'falkordb'})"
    exit
    

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/replication.html#22-verify-data-on-replica)
2.2 Verify Data on Replica

    # Connect to the replica
    docker exec -it falkordb-replica1 redis-cli
    GRAPH.RO_QUERY mygraph "MATCH (n) RETURN n"
    # Output should be:
    # 1) 1) "n"
    # 2) 1) 1) 1) 1) "id"
    #             2) (integer) 0
    #          2) 1) "labels"
    #             2) 1) "Database"
    #          3) 1) "properties"
    #             2) 1) 1) "name"
    #                   2) "falkordb"
    # 3) 1) "Cached execution: 1"
    #    2) "Query internal execution time: 0.122645 milliseconds"
    

**Expected output:** The data created on the master should be available on the replica.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/replication.html#best-practices)
Best Practices
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   **Read-Only Queries on Replicas:** Use `GRAPH.RO_QUERY` for read operations on replicas to prevent accidental writes
*   **Monitor Replication Lag:** Check replication status regularly using Redis `INFO replication` command
*   **Multiple Replicas:** Configure multiple replicas for better read scalability and redundancy
*   **Network Latency:** Place master and replicas in the same network or region for optimal performance

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/replication.html#troubleshooting)
Troubleshooting
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If replication is not working:

1.  Verify network connectivity between containers
2.  Check that the master is accessible from the replica
3.  Review logs for errors: `docker logs falkordb-replica1`

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/replication.html#next-steps)
Next Steps
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

With replication configured, FalkorDB provides high availability and data redundancy. Your data is now synchronized across multiple instances, creating a robust and fault-tolerant environment.

For horizontal scalability and distributed graph operations, explore [Clustering](https://docs.falkordb.com/operations/cluster)
.

* * *

---

# algo.SPpaths | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sppath.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sppath.html#algosppaths---shortest-path-single-pair)
`algo.SPpaths` - Shortest Path (Single Pair)
=========================================================================================================================================

The `algo.SPpaths` procedure finds the shortest paths between a **source** and a **target** node, optionally constrained by cost, path length, and the number of paths to return.

It is designed for efficient and scalable computation of paths in large graphs, using properties like distance, time, or price as weights. For example, it can be used to find the fastest driving route between two cities, the cheapest shipping option in a logistics network, or the shortest communication path in a computer network.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sppath.html#syntax)
Syntax
------------------------------------------------------------------

    CALL algo.SPpaths({
      sourceNode: <node>,
      targetNode: <node>,
      relTypes: [<relationship_type>],
      weightProp: <property>,
      costProp: <property>,         // optional
      maxCost: <int>,               // optional
      maxLen: <int>,                // optional
      relDirection: "outgoing",    // or "incoming", "both"
      pathCount: <int>              // 0 = all, 1 = single (default), n > 1 = up to n
    })
    YIELD path, pathWeight, pathCost
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sppath.html#parameters)
Parameters
--------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `sourceNode` | Node | Starting node |
| `targetNode` | Node | Destination node |
| `relTypes` | Array | List of relationship types to follow |
| `weightProp` | String | Property to minimize along the path (e.g., `dist`, `time`) |
| `costProp` | String | Property to constrain the total value (optional) |
| `maxCost` | Integer | Upper bound on total cost (optional) |
| `maxLen` | Integer | Max number of relationships in the path (optional) |
| `relDirection` | String | Traversal direction (`outgoing`, `incoming`, `both`) |
| `pathCount` | Integer | Number of paths to return (0 = all shortest, 1 = default, n = max number of results) |

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sppath.html#returns)
Returns
--------------------------------------------------------------------

| Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `path` | Path | Discovered path from source to target |
| `pathWeight` | Integer | Sum of the weightProp across the path |
| `pathCost` | Integer | Sum of the costProp across the path (if used) |

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sppath.html#examples)
Examples
----------------------------------------------------------------------

Let’s take this Road Network Graph as an example:

![Road network](https://docs.falkordb.com/images/road_network.png)

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sppath.html#example-shortest-path-by-distance-from-city-a-to-city-g)
Example: Shortest Path by Distance from City A to City G

    MATCH (a:City{name:'A'}), (g:City{name:'G'})
    CALL algo.SPpaths({
      sourceNode: a,
      targetNode: g,
      relTypes: ['Road'],
      weightProp: 'dist'
    })
    YIELD path, pathWeight
    RETURN pathWeight, [n in nodes(path) | n.name] AS pathNodes
    

#### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sppath.html#expected-result)
Expected Result:

| pathWeight | pathNodes | |————|—————| | `12` | \[A, D, E, G\] |

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sppath.html#example-bounded-cost-path-from-city-a-to-city-g)
Example: Bounded Cost Path from City A to City G

    MATCH (a:City{name:'A'}), (g:City{name:'G'})
    CALL algo.SPpaths({
      sourceNode: a,
      targetNode: g,
      relTypes: ['Road'],
      weightProp: 'dist',
      costProp: 'time',
      maxCost: 12,
      pathCount: 2
    })
    YIELD path, pathWeight, pathCost
    RETURN pathWeight, pathCost, [n in nodes(path) | n.name] AS pathNodes
    

#### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sppath.html#expected-result-1)
Expected Result:

| pathWeight | pathCost | pathNodes | |————|———-|——————| | `16` | `10` | \[A, D, F, G\] | | `14` | `12` | \[A, D, C, F, G\] |

* * *

* * *

---

# algo.SSpaths | FalkorDB Docs

[Skip to main content](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sspath.html#main-content)
 Link Menu Expand (external link) Document Search Copy Copied

[](https://www.falkordb.com/)

 Search FalkorDB Docs

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sspath.html#algosspaths---single-source-paths)
`algo.SSpaths` - Single Source Paths
===========================================================================================================================

The `algo.SSpaths` procedure returns all shortest paths from a **source node** to multiple reachable nodes, subject to constraints like cost, path length, and number of paths to return.

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sspath.html#syntax)
Syntax
------------------------------------------------------------------

    CALL algo.SSpaths({
      sourceNode: <node>,
      relTypes: [<relationship_type>],
      weightProp: <property>,         // optional
      costProp: <property>,           // optional
      maxCost: <int>,                 // optional
      maxLen: <int>,                  // optional
      relDirection: "outgoing",      // or "incoming", "both"
      pathCount: <int>
    })
    YIELD path, pathWeight, pathCost
    

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sspath.html#parameters)
Parameters
--------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `sourceNode` | Node | Starting node |
| `relTypes` | Array | List of relationship types to follow |
| `weightProp` | String | Property to minimize along the path (e.g., `dist`, `time`) |
| `costProp` | String | Property to constrain the total value (optional) |
| `maxCost` | Integer | Upper bound on total cost (optional) |
| `maxLen` | Integer | Max number of relationships in the path (optional) |
| `relDirection` | String | Traversal direction (`outgoing`, `incoming`, `both`) |
| `pathCount` | Integer | Number of paths to return (0 = all shortest, 1 = default, n = max number of results) |

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sspath.html#returns)
Returns
--------------------------------------------------------------------

| Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `path` | Path | Discovered path from source to target |
| `pathWeight` | Integer | Sum of the weightProp across the path |
| `pathCost` | Integer | Sum of the costProp across the path (if used) |

[](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sspath.html#examples)
Examples
----------------------------------------------------------------------

Let’s take this Road Network Graph as an example:

![Road network](https://docs.falkordb.com/images/road_network.png)

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sspath.html#example-all-shortest-paths-by-distance-up-to-10-km)
Example: All Shortest Paths by Distance (up to 10 km)

    MATCH (a:City{name:'A'})
    CALL algo.SSpaths({
      sourceNode: a,
      relTypes: ['Road'],
      costProp: 'dist',
      maxCost: 10,
      pathCount: 1000
    })
    YIELD path, pathCost
    RETURN pathCost, [n in nodes(path) | n.name] AS pathNodes
    ORDER BY pathCost
    

#### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sspath.html#expected-result)
Expected Result:

| pathCost | pathNodes |  
|———-| ———- | | `2` | \[A, D\] | | `3` | \[A, B\] | | `6` | \[A, D, C\] | | `7` | \[A, D, E\] | | `8` | \[A, B, D\] | | `8` | \[A, C\] | | `10` | \[A, B, E\] |

* * *

### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sspath.html#example-top-5-shortest-paths-from-a-by-distance)
Example: Top 5 Shortest Paths from A by Distance

    MATCH (a:City{name:'A'})
    CALL algo.SSpaths({
      sourceNode: a,
      relTypes: ['Road'],
      weightProp: 'dist',
      pathCount: 5
    })
    YIELD path, pathWeight, pathCost
    RETURN pathWeight, pathCost, [n in nodes(path) | n.name] AS pathNodes
    ORDER BY pathWeight
    

#### [](https://docs.falkordb.com/algorithms/sspath.html#expected-result-1)
Expected Result:

| pathWeight | pathCost | pathNodes |  
| ———–|———-| ———- | | `2` | `1` | \[A, D\] | | `3` | `1` | \[A, B\] | | `6` | `2` | \[A, D, C\] | | `7` | `2` | \[A, D, E\] | | `8` | `1` | \[A, C\] |

* * *
